figures.caption: Figure 3: Rollout-Tree visualization extracted from training-time rollouts on datalad_final_05ea.

Figure 2: Training episode resolve rate on the R2E dataset. All methods start from the same pretrained checkpoint and share the GRPO optimization framework; they differ only in how per-token advantages are computed. The progressive separation confirms that finer-grained credit assignment yields more effective policy gradients.
Table 1: Action categories classified by effect on problem-solving progress. Dedicated tools and bash commands with equivalent effects map to the same category.

Figure 5: Quadrant analysis of step-level advantage agreement between GRPO+Step and RTMC on datalad_final_05ea (4 success, 4 failure rollouts). Each bar shows the number of action steps (with percentage) falling into the four agreement quadrants. Blue bars (X−, Y+) indicate steps where RTMC assigns positive advantage while GRPO+Step assigns negative—steps RTMC rescues in failure rollouts (3%). Orange bars (X+, Y−) indicate steps GRPO+Step blindly rewards but RTMC correctly penalizes as suboptimal (15% of success-rollout steps).

Figure 1: A rollout tree from four rollouts. Rollout 1 terminates at depth 1 via a1 (failure), while rollouts 2–4 share action a2 and diverge at deeper states s1, s2. Returns G0 are discounted from terminal rewards back to s0, yielding per-action advantages.

Figure 4: Step-level advantage for each of the 8 rollouts (4 success, 4 failure) in datalad_final_05ea. Each subplot title shows the method and rollout outcome (SUCCESS/FAIL). GRPO and GRPO+Step provide coarse-grained credit assignment, while RTMC produces step-varying advantages that reflect local action quality within the rollout tree.
